care_notes: Stake or cage plants and remove side shoots weekly. Water at the base to prevent blight.
Very productive and forgiving; great for pots of at least 10 liters. Pick fruit often to encourage more trusses.
Needs warmth; in Poland it crops best in a greenhouse or sheltered sunny spot. Keep soil evenly moist to avoid blossom end rot.
Sow directly only after mid-May when soil is warm. Water generously and pick fruit every two days to keep plants productive.
One or two plants feed a family; give each a full square meter. Harvest fruit young at 15-20 cm for best flavor.
Plant on a compost-rich mound and let vines sprawl. Harvest before the first frost when the stem corks over.
Sow directly after mid-May; seeds rot in cold soil. Fixes its own nitrogen, so go easy on fertilizer.
Sow early; peas tolerate light frost and crop before summer heat. Provide twiggy supports for climbing varieties.
Needs deep, stone-free soil; thin seedlings to 5 cm. Interplant with onions to confuse carrot fly.
Sow from mid-April; each seed cluster gives several seedlings, so thin early. Harvest at tennis-ball size for tender roots.
Plant sets in April for an easy crop; stop watering once tops start to fall over. Cure bulbs in the sun before storing.
Plant cloves in late autumn for the biggest bulbs; winter cold improves bulbing. Harvest when the lower leaves yellow.
Transplant pencil-thick seedlings into deep holes and earth up for long white shanks. Stands in the ground well into late autumn.
Sow small batches every two weeks for a steady supply. Give afternoon shade in midsummer to delay bolting.
A cool-season crop: sow in early spring and again in late August. Bolts quickly in heat, so skip midsummer sowings.
Flavor improves after the first frosts, so harvest into winter. Pick outer leaves and let the center keep growing.
Cut the main head before flower buds open; side shoots will follow for weeks. Net against cabbage white butterflies.
Any check in growth ruins the curd, so never let it dry out. Bend leaves over the developing head to keep it white.
A heavy feeder: enrich the bed with compost before planting. Firm heads of late varieties store for months in a cool cellar.
Ready in four to six weeks; sow little and often. Water steadily or roots turn woody and pungent.
Chit seed potatoes indoors and plant after mid-April. Earth up stems twice to increase yield and stop tubers greening.
Needs a long season: sow indoors by early March. Keep constantly moist and remove the lowest leaves to encourage a round root.
Use fresh seed each year as it loses viability fast; germination takes up to three weeks. Frost sweetens the roots, so harvest late.
Plant in blocks rather than rows for good wind pollination. Harvest when silks turn brown and kernels release milky juice.
Firm soil well at planting and stake tall plants in autumn. Pick sprouts from the bottom up after the first frosts improve flavor.
In Poland it really needs a greenhouse or polytunnel to fruit reliably. Limit each plant to five or six fruits for decent size.
Pinch out growing tips regularly to keep plants bushy and delay flowering. Hates cold nights, so keep it indoors until late May.
Germination is slow, up to four weeks; soaking seed overnight helps. Cut outer stems first and plants resprout all season.
Sow directly where it will grow; dill resents transplanting. Repeat sowings every three weeks for continuous fresh fronds.
Bolts fast in heat; sow directly in light shade and harvest young. Let a few plants set seed for a coriander-seed harvest.
Extremely invasive: always grow in a sunken pot or container. Cut back hard in midsummer for a fresh flush of leaves.
Thrives in poor, free-draining soil and full sun; overwatering kills it. Trim lightly after flowering to keep plants compact.
Not reliably hardy in zone 6a: grow in a pot and overwinter in a bright, cool room. Let soil dry between waterings.
Plant in lean, alkaline, well-drained soil; wet winter feet are fatal. Shear by a third right after flowering, never into old wood.
Flavor is strongest just before flowering, the best moment to dry stems. Divide clumps every three years to keep them vigorous.
Prune in spring to prevent legginess and replace woody plants every four to five years. Tolerates drought once established.
Cut whole tufts to 3 cm and they regrow within weeks. Divide congested clumps in spring or autumn.
Self-seeds prolifically; cut back before seed set to keep it in bounds. Leaves are most aromatic before flowering.
True French tarragon is grown only from divisions or cuttings, never seed. Mulch the crown for winter in colder spots.
Plant runners in August for a full crop the next June. Mulch with straw to keep fruit clean and deter slugs.
Autumn-fruiting types are simplest: cut all canes to the ground each winter. Tie summer-fruiting canes to wires and remove spent ones after harvest.
Demands acidic soil at pH 3.8-4.8; plant in pure peat or acidic substrate and water with rainwater. Plant two varieties for better pollination.
Fruits best on young wood: each winter remove a quarter of the oldest stems at the base. Very hardy and reliable in Poland.
Unlike black currant it fruits on older spurs, so prune lightly. Tolerates light shade and still crops well.
Keep the center of the bush open to improve air flow and reduce mildew. Watch for sawfly larvae that can strip leaves in days.
Trees on M9 rootstock need a permanent stake and a second variety nearby for pollination. Thin fruitlets in June for larger apples.
Most sour cherries are self-fertile, so a single tree crops well. Prune right after harvest, never in winter, to avoid silver leaf disease.
Choose hardy hybrid varieties for Poland and train on a south-facing wall. Strict summer pruning is essential for ripe, healthy fruit.
Prune in early spring when forsythia blooms, cutting to outward-facing buds. Water at the base only; wet leaves invite black spot.
Never let it dry out; the name means water vessel. It flowers on old wood, so protect buds from late frosts and prune only lightly.
The queen of the shade garden, grown mainly for foliage. Slugs are the only real enemy; use barriers or beer traps from early spring.
Sow directly in late April and protect seedlings from slugs and birds. Stake tall varieties in windy spots.
Root secretions suppress soil nematodes, making it the classic vegetable-bed companion. Deadhead for non-stop bloom until frost.
Poor soil gives more flowers; rich soil gives only leaves. Works as a trap crop, luring aphids away from vegetables, and flowers are edible.
Plant tubers after mid-May and lift them after the first frost blackens foliage; store frost-free in peat. Deadhead constantly for blooms into October.
The classic Polish balcony plant; feed weekly and deadhead for endless bloom. Overwinter plants on a cool bright windowsill, nearly dry.
A heavy feeder in hanging baskets; weekly liquid feed keeps it flowering. Shelter from heavy rain, which damages the blooms.
Plant bulbs in October at three times their depth. Let foliage die down naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb.
Fully hardy and vole-proof thanks to toxic bulbs; naturalizes happily in lawns. Plant by the end of September for the best roots.
Plant with buds no deeper than 5 cm or it will not flower. Dislikes being moved; established clumps bloom for decades.
Fixes nitrogen and thrives in poor, slightly acidic soil. Cut spent spikes promptly for a second flush in late summer.
Sow directly in place; rich soil or feeding gives ferny leaves but few flowers. Self-seeds gently for next year.
Loves heat and resents root disturbance, so sow in pots and transplant carefully. The more you cut, the more it flowers.
Sow in summer for flowers the following spring, or buy plugs in March. Tolerates frost and blooms in cool weather when little else does.
Thrives in damp shade where almost nothing else grows. Leave old fronds over winter as frost protection and cut them off in March.
Leave the dried clump standing for winter interest and cut to 15 cm in early spring. Plant in spring only, so it roots before winter.
Drought-tolerant once established and beloved by bees and butterflies. Leave seed heads for goldfinches and winter structure.
Blooms tirelessly from July until frost with almost no care. Divide clumps every four years in spring.
Stake spikes early; summer storms snap them otherwise. Cut back hard after the first bloom for a second flowering in September.
Extremely hardy and tolerant of poor soil. Remove spent flower panicles right after bloom and thin old stems every few years.
Its golden bloom marks the start of the Polish gardening season. Prune immediately after flowering, since next spring's buds form on this year's wood.
Clip hedges twice a year, in June and late August. Inspect weekly for box tree moth caterpillars, now widespread across Poland.
Poland's most popular hedge conifer; water deeply in dry summers or inner foliage browns. Trim in June and September, never into bare wood.
Thrives in sandy, poor soil with virtually no care once established. Keep away from apple and pear trees, as it hosts rust diseases.
Needs acidic, humus-rich soil and a wind-sheltered spot; mulch with pine bark. Water evergreen varieties before winter, as they lose moisture on frosty sunny days.
Give it a moss pole to climb and bright indirect light for the iconic split leaves. Let the top few centimeters of soil dry between waterings.
Nearly indestructible; tolerates low light and irregular watering. Cuttings root in a glass of water within two weeks.
Thrives on neglect; overwatering is the only way to kill it. Water roughly every two weeks in summer and monthly in winter.
Produces plantlets on runners that root instantly in soil or water. Brown leaf tips usually mean fluoridated tap water; use filtered or rainwater.
Notorious for dropping leaves after any move or draft; pick one bright spot and leave it there. Keep evenly moist but never waterlogged.
Stores water in rhizomes, so it shrugs off weeks without care. Tolerates the darkest corners of any office or flat.
Dramatically droops when thirsty and recovers within hours of watering. Blooms even in low light; wipe leaves monthly to keep pores clear.
Trails or climbs happily in bright indirect light. Pinch long vines to keep the plant full, and root the cuttings in water.
Sensitive to fluoride in tap water, which browns leaf tips; water with filtered or standing water. Cut leggy canes back and they resprout below the cut.
Needs a south-facing windowsill and gritty cactus soil. Water only when the pot feels light, and barely at all in winter.
Demands high humidity, soft water, and steady warmth; crispy edges mean dry air. Folds its patterned leaves upward at night.
